title: Crash-Tested Dog Car Seats — What 'Crash-Tested' Actually Means
seoTitle: Crash-Tested Dog Car Seats – What It Actually Means (2026)
metaDescription: Most 'crash-tested' dog car seats aren't. Only CPS-certified seats pass real crash simulations. Learn what to look for and which brands actually test.
author: GetPawsy Pet Care Team

content.text: There's no regulation requiring pet car seats to be crash-tested. Any manufacturer can print 'crash-tested' on packaging without standardized proof. Unlike child car seats (regulated by FMVSS 213), dog car seats have zero federal safety standards. This means you need to know what to look for beyond marketing claims.
What Real Crash Testing Looks Like

The Center for Pet Safety (CPS) is the only independent organization that conducts standardized crash tests for pet restraints. Their testing uses instrumented crash test dog forms at 30 mph frontal impact — similar to child seat testing. Only a handful of brands have passed CPS certification. Most products on Amazon have never undergone independent testing.
Key Safety Features to Look For

Reinforced stitching rated for crash forces (not just static weight). Three-point harness attachment (not a single tether point). Padded chest plate to distribute crash forces away from the neck. LATCH or seatbelt-through anchoring system. Anti-rotation design to prevent the seat from flipping on impact.

Any seat that only uses a leash clip attachment. Seats with single-point tethers that allow the dog to launch forward. Soft-sided booster seats without rigid frame reinforcement. Products that claim 'crash-tested' without citing CPS or an equivalent lab. Remember: a seat that fails in a crash is worse than no seat — it becomes additional shrapnel.

Browse our [dog car travel safety](/dog/dog-car-travel-safety) collection for seats selected based on construction quality, restraint design, and available safety testing data. For small dogs, see our specialized [car seats for small dogs](/dog/dog-car-travel-safety/car-seats). Also read about [state travel laws](/guides/dog-travel-safety-laws-by-state) to understand the legal landscape.

faq.question: Are any dog car seats actually crash-tested?
faq.answer: Only seats certified by the Center for Pet Safety (CPS) have undergone standardized crash testing. Brands like Sleepypod and some Kurgo models have passed. Most products using 'crash-tested' in marketing have not been independently verified.

faq.question: Is a dog harness safer than a car seat?
faq.answer: For large dogs (over 30 lbs), a crash-tested harness with seatbelt attachment is generally safer than a car seat. Car seats are better for small dogs under 25 lbs because they provide containment plus elevation.

faq.question: How much should I spend on a dog car seat?
faq.answer: Quality crash-informed car seats cost $40–$120. CPS-certified options range $60–$200. Don't trust sub-$20 seats — they lack the reinforcement and attachment systems needed for real safety.
